content.text: Scenario generation is the task of producing coherent, contextually grounded narratives or structured descriptions of situations that could plausibly occur within a defined domain. LLMs excel at it because scenarios require combining constraints, world knowledge, causal reasoning, and natural language in a way that is difficult to achieve with purely rule-based or template systems. Applications range from QA test-case generation and synthetic training-data creation to business risk planning, incident-response tabletop exercises, and user-research persona journeys.
Pipeline Overview

attrs.alt: Scenario generation pipeline showing seed inputs flowing into an LLM engine that applies constrained sampling, counterfactual branching, and persona injection, producing five scenario types that then pass through four quality gates before being accepted or regenerated

content.text: Four inputs drive every generation run: a
seed context
that grounds the LLM in a specific domain and setting; explicit
constraints
(invariants that every scenario must satisfy);
personas or roles
when the scenario involves human actors; and
coverage goals

that specify which edge cases or axis combinations the batch must include. Quality gates run after generation to reject duplicates, constraint violations, or implausible outputs before they reach consumers.
Structured Scenario Representation
Ask the LLM to return structured JSON rather than prose so downstream code can validate, filter, and index scenarios without further parsing.

from __future__ import annotations
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

@dataclass
class Scenario:
    id: str
    title: str
    description: str               # 2-4 sentence narrative
    actors: list[str]              # roles involved
    preconditions: list[str]       # state before the scenario begins
    steps: list[str]               # what happens, in order
    expected_outcome: str
    tags: list[str]                # e.g. ["edge-case", "high-load"]
    metadata: dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)

@dataclass
class ScenarioSpec:
    domain: str                    # e.g. "e-commerce checkout"
    seed_context: str              # background knowledge paragraph
    constraints: list[str]         # must-hold for every scenario
    axes: dict[str, list[str]]     # variation dimensions → values
    count: int = 5
    audience: str = "software engineers"

def generate_scenarios(spec: ScenarioSpec) -> list[Scenario]:
    axis_block = "\n".join(
        f"  {axis}: {', '.join(values)}"
        for axis, values in spec.axes.items()
    )
    constraint_block = "\n".join(f"- {c}" for c in spec.constraints)

    system = (
        "You are a scenario designer. Return a JSON array of scenario objects. "
        "Each object must have these keys: id (string), title (string), "
        "description (string, 2-4 sentences), actors (array of strings), "
        "preconditions (array of strings), steps (array of strings), "
        "expected_outcome (string), tags (array of strings). "
        "Return only the JSON array — no prose, no markdown fences."
    )
    user = (
        f"Domain: {spec.domain}\n\n"
        f"Context:\n{spec.seed_context}\n\n"
        f"Constraints (every scenario must satisfy all of these):\n{constraint_block}\n\n"
        f"Variation axes (cover different combinations across the {spec.count} scenarios):\n"
        f"{axis_block}\n\n"
        f"Generate {spec.count} distinct, realistic scenarios for {spec.audience}."
    )
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=4096,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
        system=system,
    )
    raw: list[dict] = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    return [
        Scenario(
            id=item["id"],
            title=item["title"],
            description=item["description"],
            actors=item["actors"],
            preconditions=item["preconditions"],
            steps=item["steps"],
            expected_outcome=item["expected_outcome"],
            tags=item["tags"],
        )
        for item in raw
    ]
Test and QA Scenario Generation

Generating test cases is the highest-value near-term application. The LLM can reason about boundary conditions, interaction effects, and failure modes that human testers miss. Feed it the function signature, docstring, known constraints, and any already-existing test cases so it targets uncovered territory.

import ast, textwrap
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class TestScenario:
    name: str
    description: str
    inputs: dict[str, Any]
    expected_behaviour: str       # prose — what the code SHOULD do
    expected_output: str | None   # concrete return value when deterministic
    category: str                 # "happy", "boundary", "error", "security"

def generate_test_scenarios(
    function_source: str,
    existing_test_names: list[str] | None = None,
    count: int = 8,
) -> list[TestScenario]:
    """Generate test scenarios for a Python function."""
    existing_block = (
        "Already covered: " + ", ".join(existing_test_names)
        if existing_test_names
        else "No existing tests yet."
    )
    system = (
        "You are a senior QA engineer. Given Python source code, produce a JSON "
        "array of test scenario objects. Keys: name (snake_case string), "
        "description (string), inputs (object mapping param names to values), "
        "expected_behaviour (string), expected_output (string or null), "
        "category (one of: happy, boundary, error, security). "
        "Return only the JSON array."
    )
    user = (
        f"Function source:\n```python\n{function_source}\n```\n\n"
        f"{existing_block}\n\n"
        f"Generate {count} test scenarios. Prioritise boundary conditions, "
        "error paths, and security-relevant inputs over additional happy-path tests."
    )
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=3000,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
        system=system,
    )
    raw = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    return [
        TestScenario(
            name=item["name"],
            description=item["description"],
            inputs=item["inputs"],
            expected_behaviour=item["expected_behaviour"],
            expected_output=item.get("expected_output"),
            category=item["category"],
        )
        for item in raw
    ]


# Example usage
source = textwrap.dedent("""
    def parse_price(value: str) -> float:
        \"\"\"Parse a price string like '$1,234.56' or '1234.56' into a float.
        Raises ValueError for non-numeric input.
        Returns a non-negative float.\"\"\"
        cleaned = value.replace('$', '').replace(',', '').strip()
        result = float(cleaned)
        if result < 0:
            raise ValueError(f"Negative price not allowed: {result}")
        return result
""")

scenarios = generate_test_scenarios(
    source,
    existing_test_names=["test_basic_dollar_sign", "test_no_prefix"],
    count=10,
)
for s in scenarios:
    print(f"[{s.category}] {s.name}: {s.description}")
The LLM will typically surface inputs like an empty string, a string with only whitespace, values like
, extremely large floats, Unicode look-alike characters, and injection strings — categories that manually written parametrize lists miss.
Business Planning Scenarios
"What-if" business scenarios explore how a system or plan performs under different future conditions. The key design choice is making the
variables explicit
so the LLM varies them systematically rather than picking arbitrarily.

@dataclass
class BusinessScenario:
    name: str
    narrative: str
    variable_values: dict[str, str]   # axis → concrete value used
    key_risks: list[str]
    key_opportunities: list[str]
    recommended_actions: list[str]
    likelihood: str                    # "low" | "medium" | "high"
    impact: str                        # "low" | "medium" | "high" | "critical"

def generate_business_scenarios(
    company_context: str,
    variables: dict[str, list[str]],   # e.g. {"market_growth": ["+15%", "+2%", "-10%"]}
    horizon: str = "12 months",
    count: int = 6,
) -> list[BusinessScenario]:
    variable_block = "\n".join(
        f"  {var}: " + " / ".join(values)
        for var, values in variables.items()
    )
    system = (
        "You are a strategic planning consultant. Return a JSON array of scenario "
        "objects. Keys: name, narrative (2-3 paragraphs), variable_values (object), "
        "key_risks (array), key_opportunities (array), recommended_actions (array), "
        "likelihood (low/medium/high), impact (low/medium/high/critical). "
        "Return only the JSON array."
    )
    user = (
        f"Company context:\n{company_context}\n\n"
        f"Planning horizon: {horizon}\n\n"
        f"Scenario variables (pick one value from each range per scenario):\n"
        f"{variable_block}\n\n"
        f"Generate {count} distinct planning scenarios that span the range from "
        "optimistic to severe. Each scenario must state which variable value it uses."
    )
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=4096,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
        system=system,
    )
    raw = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    return [
        BusinessScenario(
            name=item["name"],
            narrative=item["narrative"],
            variable_values=item["variable_values"],
            key_risks=item["key_risks"],
            key_opportunities=item["key_opportunities"],
            recommended_actions=item["recommended_actions"],
            likelihood=item["likelihood"],
            impact=item["impact"],
        )
        for item in raw
    ]


# Example
scenarios = generate_business_scenarios(
    company_context=(
        "Mid-size SaaS company, 200 employees, $20M ARR, 85% gross margin. "
        "Primary product: B2B analytics dashboard. Key cost: cloud infrastructure."
    ),
    variables={
        "market_growth": ["+15%", "+3%", "-8%"],
        "cloud_cost_change": ["-10%", "flat", "+25%"],
        "new_competitor": ["none", "well-funded"],
        "churn_rate": ["5% annual", "12% annual", "20% annual"],
    },
    horizon="18 months",
    count=5,
)
Persona-Driven Scenario Generation
Persona injection anchors scenarios to realistic human behaviour. Rather than describing an anonymous user, you inject a fully characterised persona and ask the LLM to generate the scenario
as experienced by that person
. This is especially effective for UX research, support workflow design, and accessibility testing.

import asyncio
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Persona:
    name: str
    role: str
    technical_level: str          # "novice" | "intermediate" | "expert"
    goals: list[str]
    pain_points: list[str]
    context: str                  # situational details (device, time pressure, etc.)

@dataclass
class PersonaScenario:
    persona_name: str
    scenario_title: str
    trigger: str                  # what prompted the interaction
    journey: list[str]            # step-by-step experience
    friction_points: list[str]    # where the persona struggles
    emotion_arc: str              # frustrated → relieved, etc.
    outcome: str
    design_insights: list[str]

async def generate_persona_scenario(
    persona: Persona,
    base_situation: str,
    product_name: str,
) -> PersonaScenario:
    system = (
        "You are a UX researcher writing user journey scenarios. "
        "Return a single JSON object with keys: persona_name, scenario_title, "
        "trigger, journey (array of strings), friction_points (array), "
        "emotion_arc (string), outcome (string), design_insights (array). "
        "Reason from the persona's perspective — their skill level and goals "
        "should visibly shape what they do and where they get stuck. "
        "Return only the JSON object."
    )
    persona_block = (
        f"Name: {persona.name}\n"
        f"Role: {persona.role}\n"
        f"Technical level: {persona.technical_level}\n"
        f"Goals: {', '.join(persona.goals)}\n"
        f"Pain points: {', '.join(persona.pain_points)}\n"
        f"Context: {persona.context}"
    )
    user = (
        f"Persona:\n{persona_block}\n\n"
        f"Product: {product_name}\n"
        f"Base situation: {base_situation}\n\n"
        "Write a realistic user journey scenario for this persona."
    )
    import anthropic
    async_client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()
    response = await async_client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=2000,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
        system=system,
    )
    raw = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    return PersonaScenario(
        persona_name=persona.name,
        scenario_title=raw["scenario_title"],
        trigger=raw["trigger"],
        journey=raw["journey"],
        friction_points=raw["friction_points"],
        emotion_arc=raw["emotion_arc"],
        outcome=raw["outcome"],
        design_insights=raw["design_insights"],
    )

async def generate_all_persona_scenarios(
    personas: list[Persona],
    base_situation: str,
    product_name: str,
) -> list[PersonaScenario]:
    tasks = [
        generate_persona_scenario(p, base_situation, product_name)
        for p in personas
    ]
    return await asyncio.gather(*tasks)


# Example
personas = [
    Persona(
        name="Maria", role="Small business owner",
        technical_level="novice",
        goals=["send invoices quickly", "get paid faster"],
        pain_points=["confused by jargon", "limited time"],
        context="Mobile, between client appointments, spotty signal",
    ),
    Persona(
        name="Dev", role="Freelance developer",
        technical_level="expert",
        goals=["automate invoice workflow", "integrate with accounting API"],
        pain_points=["no webhook support", "rate limits"],
        context="Desktop, multiple browser tabs open",
    ),
]
results = asyncio.run(
    generate_all_persona_scenarios(personas, "Creating a first invoice", "InvoiceApp")
)
Counterfactual Scenario Generation
Counterfactuals ask "what would have happened if one thing were different?" They are essential for incident post-mortems, risk analysis, and model robustness testing. The technique is to describe a
base event
and then enumerate the axes you want to perturb, asking the LLM to re-simulate each variant.

@dataclass
class CounterfactualSpec:
    base_event: str                    # what actually happened
    change_axis: str                   # what is being altered
    changed_value: str                 # the counterfactual value
    domain_context: str

@dataclass
class Counterfactual:
    axis: str
    changed_value: str
    scenario: str                      # the altered narrative
    divergence_point: str              # earliest moment it differs from base
    outcome_delta: str                 # how outcome changes vs. base
    preventability: str                # could this have been caught earlier?

def generate_counterfactuals(
    base_event: str,
    domain_context: str,
    change_axes: dict[str, list[str]],  # axis → alternative values to try
) -> list[Counterfactual]:
    """Generate a counterfactual for each (axis, value) pair."""
    specs = [
        CounterfactualSpec(
            base_event=base_event,
            change_axis=axis,
            changed_value=value,
            domain_context=domain_context,
        )
        for axis, values in change_axes.items()
        for value in values
    ]

    results: list[Counterfactual] = []
    for spec in specs:
        system = (
            "You are a root-cause analyst. Return a JSON object with keys: "
            "axis (string), changed_value (string), scenario (2-3 paragraph narrative), "
            "divergence_point (string), outcome_delta (string), "
            "preventability (string). Return only the JSON object."
        )
        user = (
            f"Domain context:\n{spec.domain_context}\n\n"
            f"Base event (what actually happened):\n{spec.base_event}\n\n"
            f"Counterfactual change: '{spec.change_axis}' is changed to '{spec.changed_value}'\n\n"
            "Simulate how the sequence of events would have unfolded differently. "
            "Identify the earliest divergence point and how the final outcome changes."
        )
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-8",
            max_tokens=1500,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
            system=system,
        )
        raw = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
        results.append(
            Counterfactual(
                axis=spec.change_axis,
                changed_value=spec.changed_value,
                scenario=raw["scenario"],
                divergence_point=raw["divergence_point"],
                outcome_delta=raw["outcome_delta"],
                preventability=raw["preventability"],
            )
        )
    return results


# Example: incident post-mortem analysis
counterfactuals = generate_counterfactuals(
    base_event=(
        "At 14:32 UTC a database migration script ran on production. "
        "It locked the users table for 8 minutes, causing 503 errors for all "
        "login attempts. On-call engineer was paged at 14:38 and rolled back at 14:40."
    ),
    domain_context="SaaS web application, 50,000 daily active users, PostgreSQL on RDS",
    change_axes={
        "migration_timing": ["ran during off-peak hours (02:00 UTC)"],
        "lock_strategy":    ["used LOCK NOWAIT instead of default lock"],
        "staging_test":     ["ran against production-size staging copy first"],
        "alerting_lag":     ["alert fired at 14:33 instead of 14:38"],
    },
)
Tabletop Exercise and Simulation Scenarios

Tabletop exercises walk a team through a simulated incident or crisis to practice their response procedures. LLMs can generate multi-turn, branching simulations where each team decision opens a new phase. The key technique is to author an
inject script
— a time-ordered sequence of events the LLM reveals one by one, adapting subsequent injects based on the team's declared actions.

@dataclass
class TabletopInject:
    time_offset_minutes: int      # minutes into exercise
    event: str                    # what the team learns now
    decision_prompt: str          # what they must decide
    escalation_trigger: str       # what action escalates to next phase

@dataclass
class TabletopExercise:
    title: str
    scenario_type: str            # "cybersecurity" | "ops-outage" | "supply-chain" | ...
    objective: str
    background: str
    injects: list[TabletopInject]
    debrief_questions: list[str]

def generate_tabletop_exercise(
    scenario_type: str,
    organization_context: str,
    duration_minutes: int = 90,
    participant_roles: list[str] | None = None,
) -> TabletopExercise:
    roles_block = (
        "Participant roles: " + ", ".join(participant_roles)
        if participant_roles
        else "Roles: standard incident response team"
    )
    system = (
        "You are a crisis simulation designer. Return a JSON object with keys: "
        "title (string), scenario_type (string), objective (string), "
        "background (string, 2 paragraphs), "
        "injects (array of objects each with: time_offset_minutes (int), event (string), "
        "decision_prompt (string), escalation_trigger (string)), "
        "debrief_questions (array of strings, 5-7 items). "
        "Return only the JSON object."
    )
    user = (
        f"Scenario type: {scenario_type}\n"
        f"Organization context: {organization_context}\n"
        f"{roles_block}\n"
        f"Exercise duration: {duration_minutes} minutes\n\n"
        f"Design a realistic tabletop exercise with 5-7 injects spread across "
        f"the {duration_minutes}-minute window. Each inject should escalate "
        "the situation or reveal new information that changes the team's priorities. "
        "Include a debrief question set that surfaces procedural gaps."
    )
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=3000,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
        system=system,
    )
    raw = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    return TabletopExercise(
        title=raw["title"],
        scenario_type=raw["scenario_type"],
        objective=raw["objective"],
        background=raw["background"],
        injects=[
            TabletopInject(
                time_offset_minutes=i["time_offset_minutes"],
                event=i["event"],
                decision_prompt=i["decision_prompt"],
                escalation_trigger=i["escalation_trigger"],
            )
            for i in raw["injects"]
        ],
        debrief_questions=raw["debrief_questions"],
    )


exercise = generate_tabletop_exercise(
    scenario_type="ransomware attack",
    organization_context="Regional hospital network, 3 campuses, Epic EHR system",
    duration_minutes=90,
    participant_roles=["CISO", "CIO", "Legal counsel", "Head of nursing operations", "PR lead"],
)
Diversity Checking and Coverage Tracking
Without explicit diversity controls, LLMs tend to generate scenarios that cluster around the most prototypical case. Two complementary mechanisms prevent this:
string-similarity deduplication
for cheap near-duplicate detection, and
axis-combination coverage tracking
to ensure the batch spans the intended variation space.
Synthetic Training Data Scenarios
Fine-tuning and RLHF pipelines need large, diverse labelled datasets. LLMs can generate
input–output pairs
that simulate rare or expensive-to-collect situations. The discipline is to define the
distribution
you need rather than just asking for random examples — otherwise the generated corpus will mirror the same biases as the generating model.

from dataclasses import dataclass
import random

@dataclass
class TrainingExample:
    input: str
    output: str
    label: str | None = None     # classification label if applicable
    difficulty: str = "medium"   # "easy" | "medium" | "hard"
    source_axes: dict[str, str] = None  # which axis values were used

def generate_training_examples(
    task_description: str,
    input_output_spec: str,         # what input looks like, what output should be
    distribution: dict[str, list[str]],  # axis → levels to vary
    few_shots: list[dict] | None = None, # list of {input, output} gold examples
    count: int = 20,
) -> list[TrainingExample]:
    """Generate synthetic training examples for a fine-tuning dataset."""
    few_shot_block = ""
    if few_shots:
        examples_text = "\n\n".join(
            f"Input: {ex['input']}\nOutput: {ex['output']}"
            for ex in few_shots
        )
        few_shot_block = f"Gold examples (match this quality):\n{examples_text}\n\n"

    distribution_block = "\n".join(
        f"  {axis}: {', '.join(levels)}"
        for axis, levels in distribution.items()
    )

    system = (
        "You are a dataset curator. Return a JSON array of training example objects. "
        "Each object has keys: input (string), output (string), "
        "difficulty (easy/medium/hard), source_axes (object mapping axis → value used). "
        "The examples must be diverse — spread across all listed distribution axes. "
        "Return only the JSON array."
    )
    user = (
        f"Task: {task_description}\n\n"
        f"Input/Output specification:\n{input_output_spec}\n\n"
        f"{few_shot_block}"
        f"Distribution axes to vary (spread {count} examples across these):\n"
        f"{distribution_block}\n\n"
        f"Generate {count} training examples. Avoid near-duplicate inputs. "
        "Include hard examples (ambiguous, edge-case, adversarial) not just easy ones."
    )
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=6000,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
        system=system,
    )
    raw = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    return [
        TrainingExample(
            input=item["input"],
            output=item["output"],
            difficulty=item.get("difficulty", "medium"),
            source_axes=item.get("source_axes"),
        )
        for item in raw
    ]


# Example: customer support intent classification dataset
examples = generate_training_examples(
    task_description="Classify customer support messages by intent",
    input_output_spec=(
        "Input: a raw customer message (1-4 sentences). "
        "Output: one of [billing_dispute, feature_request, bug_report, "
        "cancellation, general_inquiry]."
    ),
    distribution={
        "tone":       ["angry", "polite", "confused", "urgent"],
        "channel":    ["email", "live_chat", "in-app"],
        "complexity": ["simple", "multi-issue", "ambiguous"],
        "intent":     ["billing_dispute", "feature_request", "bug_report",
                       "cancellation", "general_inquiry"],
    },
    few_shots=[
        {"input": "I was charged twice this month!",
         "output": "billing_dispute"},
        {"input": "Would love dark mode please",
         "output": "feature_request"},
    ],
    count=50,
)
Constraint Validation and Quality Gate
Generated scenarios must pass validation before being handed to consumers. Rather than relying on the generating LLM to self-check, run a separate
critic
call with a different system prompt that explicitly tries to find constraint violations.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ScenarioValidation:
    passed: bool
    violations: list[str]          # constraint violations found
    plausibility_score: float      # 0.0–1.0
    diversity_flags: list[str]     # axis values not represented
    suggested_fix: str | None

def validate_scenario(
    scenario: Scenario,
    constraints: list[str],
    domain_context: str,
) -> ScenarioValidation:
    """Critic pass: check a scenario against constraints and plausibility."""
    constraint_block = "\n".join(f"- {c}" for c in constraints)
    scenario_text = (
        f"Title: {scenario.title}\n"
        f"Description: {scenario.description}\n"
        f"Steps:\n" + "\n".join(f"  {i+1}. {s}" for i, s in enumerate(scenario.steps)) +
        f"\nExpected outcome: {scenario.expected_outcome}"
    )
    system = (
        "You are a quality assurance reviewer for generated scenarios. "
        "Examine the scenario critically and return a JSON object with keys: "
        "passed (bool), violations (array of strings describing constraint failures), "
        "plausibility_score (float 0.0-1.0 — how realistic is this within the domain), "
        "diversity_flags (array of strings — any axis values conspicuously absent), "
        "suggested_fix (string or null — how to fix the worst violation). "
        "Be strict: flag any scenario that could not plausibly occur in the domain."
    )
    user = (
        f"Domain context: {domain_context}\n\n"
        f"Required constraints (ALL must hold):\n{constraint_block}\n\n"
        f"Scenario to validate:\n{scenario_text}"
    )
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=800,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user}],
        system=system,
    )
    raw = json.loads(response.content[0].text)
    return ScenarioValidation(
        passed=raw["passed"],
        violations=raw["violations"],
        plausibility_score=raw["plausibility_score"],
        diversity_flags=raw["diversity_flags"],
        suggested_fix=raw.get("suggested_fix"),
    )

def validate_and_filter(
    scenarios: list[Scenario],
    constraints: list[str],
    domain_context: str,
    min_plausibility: float = 0.7,
) -> tuple[list[Scenario], list[tuple[Scenario, ScenarioValidation]]]:
    """Return (accepted, rejected_with_reasons)."""
    accepted, rejected = [], []
    for s in scenarios:
        result = validate_scenario(s, constraints, domain_context)
        if result.passed and result.plausibility_score >= min_plausibility:
            accepted.append(s)
        else:
            rejected.append((s, result))
    return accepted, rejected
Comparison: Scenario Generation Approaches
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Best for
Rule-based
Deterministic, exhaustive, auditable
Combinatorial explosion, brittle for open domains
Closed-world domains with finite state (e.g. form validation)
Template-based
Consistent structure, slot-filling is fast
Rigid, scenarios feel formulaic, no emergent detail
High-volume structured data where variety is not critical
LLM unconstrained
Fluent, creative, handles open domains
Clusters near prototypes, may violate constraints, no coverage guarantee
Brainstorming, first drafts without coverage requirements
LLM + constraints + coverage tracker
Fluent, constrained, measurable completeness
Higher token cost, requires axis taxonomy upfront
QA datasets, training data, risk planning where coverage matters
Key Design Principles
Make axes explicit.
"Generate diverse scenarios" produces clustering. "Cover each combination of (load: low/medium/high) × (error: none/transient/permanent)" produces systematic spread.
Separate generator from critic.
Asking the same call to generate and self-validate is unreliable. Run a dedicated critic prompt with a different system instruction and trust its violations over the generator's assertions.
Request structured output.
JSON scenarios are immediately machine-readable for filtering, deduplication, indexing, and downstream tooling. Prose scenarios require fragile parsing.
Generate in small batches, not one giant call.
Asking for 50 scenarios at once amplifies any prompt bias. Generating 5–8 at a time with updated coverage hints after each batch yields more varied results.
Include few-shot gold examples for training data tasks.
Even 2–3 exemplars in the prompt sharply improve output quality for domain-specific vocabulary and output format.
Track scenario hashes to avoid perpetual regeneration.
When a coverage-driven loop reruns, reloading previously generated and accepted scenarios into
seen_descriptions
prevents the loop from restarting from scratch.
